behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files are not scanned
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is: code processes a file that wasn't in the changed set. This is deterministic logic that can be tested by mocking the GitHub/git API to return a specific set of changed files, then verifying the analyzer is called only with those files. A pure mock of the file-fetcher and the analyzer's input list is sufficient; we don't need real git state or real GitHub API.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the GitHub API (or git diff) to return a fixed set of changed files (e.g., ['src/foo.ts', 'src/bar.ts']). Mock the static analyzer to capture which files it receives. Run the lint step, then verify that the analyzer was invoked with exactly the changed files and no others.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment
behaviors.justification: The grouping algorithm is deterministic and local to the system. The real failure is: findings are not grouped correctly, or appear in the wrong order/section. This can be tested by creating mock findings with mixed severities and verifying the output groups them correctly. Posting to real GitHub is orthogonal to the grouping logic and can be mocked.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock findings with mixed severities (e.g., 3 errors, 2 warnings, 1 info). Pass them through the grouping and comment-formatting logic. Verify the output has sections for each severity in order (errors first, then warnings, then info), and verify findings appear in their correct sections with proper formatting.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout
behaviors.justification: Performance is non-deterministic and depends on real I/O, subprocess overhead, and analysis time. Mocks would hide performance bottlenecks—a mocked file-read and mocked analyzer are instant, so tests would always pass even if real execution times out. The real failure boundary is actual elapsed time with realistic file sizes and count. This requires System-level testing with real or realistic-scale test data and actual analysis tools.
behaviors.test_description: Create or generate a test directory with 200 real files of realistic size and complexity. Run the linter step in a CI environment (or Docker container simulating CI), measuring total elapsed time from start to finish of all file I/O and analysis. Verify the entire process (including file reading, analysis, grouping, and comment generation) completes in under 10 minutes. Verify all 200 files are analyzed.

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results posted
behaviors.justification: The real failure is: the process crashes, and remaining files are NOT analyzed. This can be tested at Integration level by mocking the linter to raise an exception or return a failure code on one file, then verifying that the error is caught, processing continues, and remaining files are analyzed with partial results posted. System-level testing with a real linter crashing on malformed input would be more thorough but isn't required for the error-handling logic itself.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test dataset with one malformed file (invalid syntax that would crash a real linter) and several valid files. Mock the linter to raise an exception or return a non-zero exit code when processing the malformed file. Run the lint step, verify that the exception is caught, remaining files continue to be analyzed, and results are posted (with the malformed file skipped or noted as an error).

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output
behaviors.justification: Deduplication is a pure data transformation—deterministic logic with no I/O or external dependencies. The real failure is: duplicate findings remain in the output. This can be caught by creating mock findings with duplicates (same file, line, and message from two different rules) and verifying only one copy appears in the output. No integration or system-level concerns exist.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock findings where two different linter rules (e.g., rule-A and rule-B) flag the same file and line with the same message (e.g., 'unused variable' on line 5 of src/foo.ts). Pass these findings through the deduplication logic, verify the output contains exactly one instance of that finding (preserving one rule name or combining them), and verify no duplicates remain.
